title: Proverbs 30
subtitle: Agur's Confession; Add Nothing to God's Words; The Four Things; The Way of an Eagle

sections.header: Verses 1–14 — Agur's Confession; Add Nothing to God's Words; Remove Falsehood and Poverty

heb.paragraph: The words of Agur son of Jakeh — the oracle (hammaśśāʾ). Agur is unknown outside this chapter; his name means 'gatherer' or 'collector.' His confession of ignorance (vv. 2–4) is the most epistemologically humble passage in Proverbs: 'I am too stupid to be a man; I do not have human understanding.' This is wisdom at its deepest — knowing the limits of knowing.

Every word of God is ṣĕrûpâ — refined, purified (v. 5). The same metallurgical language used of God testing hearts (17:3) is now applied to God's own word: it has been through the refining fire and emerged pure, without alloy. Adding to it (v. 6) is therefore both unnecessary and presumptuous — you do not improve on refined gold.

panels.ctx: The first half of ch.30 introduces a new voice: Agur son of Jakeh, who opens with an unprecedented confession of intellectual humility (vv.2-3) before asserting the sufficiency of God's word (v.5) and the prayer for neither poverty nor riches (vv.8-9). The numerical sayings begin (vv.11-14) with the four generations who curse or fail to honour.

cross.note: God's speech from the whirlwind — Agur's questions about who has gone up to heaven (v.4) echo the divine interrogation of Job. The unfathomability of God is the prerequisite for genuine humility.
Every word of God is flawless; do not add to his words — the warning against supplementing God's word with human additions.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Agur's confession -- Alter notes the opening questions are unanswerable, designed to produce the admission: I do not know. The confession of ignorance is not mere humility but epistemological accuracy. The rhetorical questions about divine transcendence leave the creature with no response but silence.

The two-petition prayer -- Alter notes Agur's prayer is the simplest and most honest in Proverbs: give me neither poverty nor riches; give me only my daily bread. The prayer asks for the exact provision that neutralises the spiritual dangers of both extremes.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Agur's confession of ignorance is Proverbs' most theologically humble passage. Waltke notes the opening question -- 'who has gone up to heaven and come down? who has cupped the wind in his hands? what is his name, and the name of his son?' -- is the OT's most concentrated expression of divine transcendence and human limitation. The mystery of the divine name and son anticipates NT Christological questions.

Add nothing to God's words lest he rebuke you -- the canonical warning following Agur's confession. Waltke notes this is placed after an extended statement of human ignorance: precisely because we know so little, we must not supplement what God has revealed with what we have invented.

Calvin: Who has gone up to heaven and come down? The questions emphasise the infinite distance between God and humanity. Only the incarnate Son has descended from and ascended to heaven -- the verse contains an implicit Christological trajectory that John 3:13 makes explicit.

Calvin: Give me neither poverty nor riches -- this is the model prayer of contentment. The man who is too rich forgets God; the man who is too poor may steal and dishonour God's name. The prayer for the middle way is the prayer of wisdom.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: the name-and-son questions discuss the divine name YHWH (whose name is he?) and the son (what is his son's name?) -- the note traces how these questions set up the revelation theology of the OT and have been read Christologically by the NT and church fathers.
NET: 'add nothing to his words' -- the note traces the canonical warning through Deut 4:2 and Rev 22:18-19. The prohibition against addition is the canonical claim to completeness and sufficiency.

sections.header: Verses 15–33 — The Four Things; The Way of an Eagle; Locusts Without a King

heb.paragraph: The ʿălûqâ (leech) has two daughters: 'Give! Give!' (v. 15). This enigmatic opening to the numerical proverbs uses the bloodsucking leech as an image of insatiability. The 'two daughters' are its twin suckers. What follows — four things that are never satisfied — develops the theme: Sheol, the barren womb, parched earth, and fire.

Three things are too wonderful for me, four I do not understand: the derek of an eagle in the sky, of a serpent on a rock, of a ship on the sea, and of a man with a young woman (vv. 18–19). Derek means both physical path and manner of movement. The common thread is that all four leave no trace — they move through their medium and the evidence of their passage vanishes. Mystery, not explanation, is the final word.

panels.ctx: The second half of ch.30 is dominated by the numerical sayings — the four things, the way of an eagle (vv.18-19), the earth shaking at four things (vv.21-23), the four small creatures who are wise (vv.24-28), and the four stately creatures (vv.29-31). The numerical form is a teaching device: cataloguing wisdom makes it memorable. The chapter closes with the warning against stirring up anger (v.33).

cross.note: Go to the ant, you sluggard — the ants as a wisdom example recurs in v.25. Small creatures with great wisdom are a consistent Proverbs motif.
Do not worry about food and clothing — Jesus's teaching embodies the middle way between poverty and riches that Agur's prayer (vv.8-9) requests.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The numerical proverbs -- Alter notes the X/X+1 form is playful and philosophical simultaneously. The categories resist systematic organisation; the observations are keen and specific. The chapter's second half is Proverbs' most empirical section.

The four small-but-wise creatures -- ants, rock badgers, locusts, lizard. Alter notes each is characterised by wisdom that exceeds its size. The ant stores provision; the rock badger builds in rock; the locusts advance in formation; the lizard enters kings' palaces. The lesson: wisdom is not a function of power or size but of well-adapted intelligence.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The numerical proverbs of vv.15-33 (three things, four things) are Proverbs' most distinctive literary form in ch.30. Waltke notes the X/X+1 pattern creates suspense and emphasis: the climactic item (the fourth thing) is always the surprise. The observations range from the unsatisfied (vv.15-16) to the mysterious (vv.18-19) to the intolerable (v.21-23) to the small-but-wise (vv.24-28).

The way of an eagle, a snake, a ship, a man with a maiden -- the four mysterious things that leave no trace. Waltke notes the series ends with the most mysterious of all: the way of a man with a maiden. The verse has often been read as about the mystery of human attraction, but the context (things that leave no trace) may suggest something about the inscrutability of relationship.

Calvin: What is his son's name? -- this anticipates the full revelation of the New Testament: the Son of God revealed in Christ. The verse does not predict Christ explicitly but opens the question that the NT answers.

Calvin: The four small wise creatures teach that wisdom and greatness are not the same. God uses small things to shame the great and weak things to shame the strong (1 Cor 1:27). Observe the ant and become wise.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: the four mysterious things that leave no trace -- the note discusses whether the fourth item (man with a maiden) refers to physical passion, psychological inscrutability, or the absence of a traceable trajectory. The mystery is the point.

NET: the four small-but-wise creatures -- the note discusses each creature's specific wisdom strategy: the ant's provision-storing, the rock badger's fortified dwelling, the locusts' coordinated advance, the lizard's access to power.

ppl.name: The righteous
ppl.text: Integrity is both compass and shield.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Give me neither poverty nor riches, but give me only my daily bread."NoteAgur's prayer for the middle way — the most psychologically honest assessment of wealth's spiritual danger in Proverbs. Both extremes threaten the relationship with God.

src.title: Ancient Near Eastern wisdom
src.quote: These proverbs reflect Israel's engagement with international wisdom traditions, especially Egyptian.
src.note: Wisdom was a shared human enterprise across cultures.

src.title: Hezekian editorial work
src.quote: Proverbs 25:1 notes that men of Hezekiah copied these proverbs — one of the most historically specific attributions in Proverbs.
src.note: The editorial activity shows Proverbs as a living, curated collection.

rec.text: Proverbs 21-30 has been foundational to Christian virtue ethics — the practical outworking of the fear of the LORD.
These chapters have been widely cited in ethics, governance, and social teaching.

rows.label: Agur's Confession: Wisdom Includes Knowing Its Limits
rows.text: I am weary, God, but I can prevail. Surely I am only a brute, not a man; I do not have human understanding. Agur's confession of ignorance is Proverbs' most theologically humble passage -- wisdom includes knowing what wisdom cannot reach.

rows.label: The Small-but-Wise Creatures
rows.text: Four things are small on the earth, yet they are exceedingly wise. The ant, rock badger, locust, and lizard -- each demonstrates wisdom that exceeds its size. Wisdom is not a function of power but of well-adapted intelligence.

tx.title: Agur's oracle — the most textually difficult passage in Proverbs
tx.content: MT reads 'The oracle of Agur son of Jakeh — the inspired utterance' (hammaśśāʾ). The text of vv. 1–4 is extraordinarily difficult; words like ʾîtîʾēl and ʾûkāl may be names, verbs, or phrases. The LXX interprets rather than translates, producing a substantially different reading. Targum Proverbs paraphrases freely.
tx.note: This passage has defeated translators ancient and modern. The names (if they are names) are unattested elsewhere; the syntax is broken; and the relationship between the oracle and the numerical sayings that follow is unclear.

tx.title: Position of chapters 30–31 in LXX
tx.content: In the LXX, the sayings of Agur (ch. 30) and Lemuel (ch. 31) do not appear at the end of the book as in MT. Instead, they are inserted after 24:22, with the Hezekian collection (25–29) following. This dramatic rearrangement means the LXX and MT present the book's conclusion entirely differently.
tx.note: The LXX order places Solomon's proverbs last, ending the book with the king's voice. MT ends with Lemuel's mother and the 'woman of valor' — a deliberately non-royal conclusion. Both arrangements are theologically meaningful.

debate.title: Agur's Confession and the Theology of Divine Transcendence

positions.name: Genuine theological humility before divine transcendence
positions.argument: Agur's confession is not false modesty but accurate epistemology: human beings do not have direct access to the divine wisdom that made the cosmos. The questions (who ascended? who cupped the wind?) are genuinely unanswerable from the human side.

positions.name: A rhetorical setup for the canonical affirmation (vv.5-6)
positions.argument: The confession of ignorance sets up the canonical claim that God's words are flawless and sufficient. The point is not perpetual agnosticism but the recognition that what we know, we know because God has revealed it.
